name: Halifax
summary: Halifax is the biggest city in Atlantic Canada, perched around a huge harbour where cruise ships dock right downtown. You can climb the star-shaped Citadel hill above the city, hear cannons boom at noon, visit Titanic survivors' artifacts and graves, and snap photos at Peggy's Cove lighthouse. Don't leave without trying a donair, the city's wonderfully messy official food.
funFacts: Halifax has one of the world's largest natural harbours, deep enough for giant cruise ships and warships to glide right up to the downtown boardwalk.
When the Titanic sank in 1912, rescue ships brought victims to Halifax. Today 121 are buried at Fairview Lawn Cemetery, more than anywhere else on Earth.
Soldiers have fired the Citadel's noon gun almost every single day since 1857. You can set your watch by the boom, which echoes across the whole harbour.
In 1917 a ship loaded with explosives blew up in Halifax Harbour. It was the biggest human-made blast before the atomic bomb, shattering windows 80 km away.
The donair, Halifax's official food, was invented here in the 1970s. It's spiced beef wrapped in flatbread with a sweet, garlicky sauce made from condensed milk.
thingsToDo: Climb Citadel Hill to explore the star-shaped Fort George, watch kilted Highlanders drill, and cover your ears for the famous noon gun cannon firing.
Visit the Maritime Museum of the Atlantic on the waterfront to see real Titanic deckchairs, the Unknown Child's tiny shoes, and Halifax Explosion artifacts.
Take a 50-minute drive or tour bus to Peggy's Cove to scramble across pink granite boulders around Canada's most photographed lighthouse.
Wander the flowerbeds, fountains, and bandstand at Halifax Public Gardens, a Victorian park opened in 1867 just minutes from the cruise terminal.
Step inside Pier 21, where nearly one million immigrants first set foot in Canada between 1928 and 1971, and find their stories on giant interactive screens.
Stroll the harbourwalk boardwalk for ice cream, buskers, and a saltwater wave-machine, then grab a donair from a classic spot like Tony's or King of Donair.

localWord.meaning: Hello in Mi'kmaq, the language of Nova Scotia's Indigenous people

images.alt: Sunset glowing pink and gold over Halifax Harbour with the city skyline reflected in the calm water.
The star-shaped stone walls of the historic Halifax Citadel fortress sitting on top of a green hill.
The bright red and white Peggy's Cove lighthouse standing on smooth granite rocks beside the blue Atlantic Ocean.
The colourful Victorian gates at the entrance of the leafy Halifax Public Gardens.
The Maritime Museum of the Atlantic on the Halifax boardwalk, with a tall ship moored alongside.
